themes: mother-daughter bonding moments and multi-generational family scenes
gift-giving and thoughtful token-of-appreciation content
restaurant and home dining experiences hosted for mothers
beauty and personal care gifting (perfume, skincare, abaya, jewelry)
emotional tribute and heartfelt message content
Saudi mother archetypes — the matriarch at home, the modern Saudi mother, the grandmother as household anchor
dos: lead with genuine emotional resonance — Arab Mother's Day drives some of the highest organic emotional engagement of any occasion in KSA
feature real or realistic multi-generational Saudi family scenes (grandmother, mother, daughter together)
use warm, soft lighting and intimate domestic or restaurant settings
frame gifts as expressions of love, not transactional purchases — the emotional wrapper is what converts on this day
produce Arabic-primary copy with emotionally resonant phrasing; classical Arabic emotional expressions ("يا أغلى إنسان" etc.) perform strongly
show the act of gifting — the moment of giving, the expression on the mother's face, the unwrapping ritual
donts: do NOT conflate Arab Mother's Day (March 21) with Western Mother's Day (second Sunday of May) — running a May campaign for an Arab audience is a noticeable error
do NOT feature only Western-style gift aesthetics (roses in Western paper, birthday-style cakes) without Saudi cultural grounding
do NOT use promotional language that overshadows the emotional moment — "BUY NOW 30% OFF" framing kills the emotional resonance that drives the day
do NOT show mothers in contexts that would be considered inappropriate or undignified in Saudi culture
do NOT ignore grandmothers and elder mothers in the visual framing — in Saudi families, the grandmother is often the emotional center
do NOT produce generic emotion-free product-catalog posts; this day requires warmth-first content even for hard-retail brands
arab_mothers_day.commercial_note: high — peak gifting day for beauty, F&B, fashion, jewelry in Q1

moments: Families gathering at a favorite local restaurant for a special brunch, daughters presenting their mothers with a surprise dessert platter
Sons and daughters preparing a homemade dinner, complete with traditional dishes that mom loves
Cafes offering complimentary coffee or tea to mothers who visit with their children on this day
f_and_b.product_role: F&B offerings enhance family gatherings and create memorable dining experiences.
timing_peaks: Brunch and dinner times on Mother's Day

moments: Daughters gifting their mothers a luxurious perfume set during a morning breakfast surprise
Mothers receiving a skincare spa voucher from their children, used for a relaxing afternoon
Granddaughters helping their grandmothers apply a new shade of lipstick, creating a bonding moment
beauty_personal_care.product_role: Beauty products serve as thoughtful gifts that pamper and celebrate mothers.
timing_peaks: Morning gift exchanges and afternoon spa visits

moments: Families visiting malls to pick out personalized gifts like customized photo frames or engraved jewelry
Children crafting handmade cards and pairing them with small retail gifts like scented candles
Retail stores hosting special Mother's Day events with live music and gift wrapping stations
retail_lifestyle.product_role: Retail products provide diverse gift options that cater to personal tastes.
timing_peaks: Afternoon and early evening shopping trips

moments: Sons surprising their mothers with a new abaya that they've been eyeing, during a family lunch
Daughters organizing a fashion show at home, showcasing new outfits they've bought for their mothers
Mothers trying on new jewelry pieces gifted by their children, during a family gathering
fashion.product_role: Fashion items are cherished gifts that enhance personal style and create lasting memories.

moments: Children booking a wellness retreat or spa day for their mothers, as a way to unwind and relax
Mothers receiving a gift of a yoga class subscription from their children, to enjoy during their morning routine
Families organizing a wellness-themed picnic in the park, complete with healthy snacks and mindfulness activities
healthcare_wellness.product_role: Wellness gifts promote relaxation and well-being for mothers.
timing_peaks: Morning wellness activities and afternoon relaxation

moments: Families considering a new home upgrade as a long-term gift for the mother, discussing options during a family dinner
Children surprising their mothers with a garden makeover, transforming it into a serene space for relaxation
Mothers receiving a home decor consultation voucher from their children, to personalize their living space
real_estate.product_role: Real estate and home improvements offer meaningful, lasting gifts that enhance living environments.
timing_peaks: Evening family discussions and weekend home visits

themes: Hajj reverence and pilgrimage imagery (respectful, never staged)
the story of Ibrahim — sacrifice, faith, family
qurban (sacrifice) meat sharing with neighbors and the needy
generosity and giving
extended family hosting
dos: frame the meat-sharing ritual (qurban) as community giving, not as a meal aesthetic
show Hajj-aligned reverence (Mecca/Kaaba imagery only when authentic and respectful, never as backdrop)
lean on extended-family hospitality scenes
use a respectful, less-celebratory tone than Eid Al-Fitr — Adha is reflective-generous, not party-joyful
Arabic-primary copy; MSA acceptable here given religious weight
donts: do NOT show graphic slaughter or animal distress imagery — never; auto-block
do NOT use Hajj imagery as a marketing backdrop (Kaaba, Mecca, pilgrim crowds) — these are reverent contexts, not stages
do NOT depict alcohol, gambling, or entertainment — entire entertainment sector is blacked out from posting during Eid Al-Adha
do NOT recycle Eid Al-Fitr creative — different occasion, different tone
do NOT trivialize the sacrifice narrative with puns or wordplay
do NOT push aggressive sales — this is a religious-reflective period
eid_al_adha.commercial_note: moderate — F&B (meat, hospitality) high; retail moderate; entertainment blacked out

Neighbors exchange plates of qurban meat, each dish a gesture of goodwill and community spirit — WHO: Neighbors, WHEN: Throughout Eid Day
f_and_b.product_role: F&B products are the centerpiece of Eid gatherings, facilitating moments of sharing and celebration.
timing_peaks: Eid Day morning for meal preparations
Eid Day lunch for family gatherings
Throughout Eid Day for neighborly exchanges

Gift exchanges of beauty sets and perfumes, a gesture of appreciation and care — WHO: Friends and family, WHEN: Throughout Eid

Community health initiatives promote wellness and safety during large gatherings — WHO: Community health workers, WHEN: Eid Day

moments: Families host extended relatives in their homes, showcasing the space and hospitality — WHO: Homeowners, WHEN: Eid Day
Prospective buyers explore properties with spacious living areas, ideal for hosting large family gatherings — WHO: Prospective homebuyers, WHEN: Weeks leading up to Eid
Real estate agents highlight properties with features that enhance festive living, such as large dining areas and guest rooms — WHO: Real estate agents, WHEN: Eid season

themes: joy and celebration after the fast
Eid clothes (new outfits, traditional thobes/abayas)
family gatherings and hospitality (kahwa, dates, ma'amoul, ka'ak)
gift-giving (Eidiya — cash gifts to children)
community Eid prayers and post-prayer gatherings
children's joy and intergenerational moments
dos: lead with celebratory warmth — bright, joyful, family-centered framing
show traditional Eid sweets (ma'amoul, ka'ak, basbousa) with Arabic naming
feature multigenerational family scenes — grandparents, parents, children together
use Eid Mubarak / كل عام وأنتم بخير openers respectfully and varied — don't repeat one phrase
highlight new clothes and gift-giving genuinely; Eid clothes are a cultural ritual not just a sale
show community: Eid prayer, post-prayer hugs, neighbor visits
donts: do NOT use 'sale ends in 2 hours' urgency on Eid Day 1 — Eid Day 1 is family, not transactional
do NOT depict alcohol, gambling, or any prohibited imagery (perennial; especially visible on Eid)
do NOT show isolated celebration — Eid is communal; lone scenes feel off
do NOT mix Eid Al-Fitr and Eid Al-Adha in the same creative without distinction — they are different events
do NOT use 'Eid Mubarak' as the only caption text — bilingual sibling expected
do NOT recycle Ramadan creative — Eid voice is celebratory-bright; Ramadan was contemplative-warm
eid_al_fitr.commercial_note: peak retail moment — Eid clothes, gifts, gift-card spend

moments: Families gather around a lavishly set table, enjoying a special Eid feast that includes exclusive dishes available only during this period.
Early morning breakfast after Eid prayers, where families indulge in traditional sweets and kahwa.
Evening gatherings where friends and family share a meal, often featuring promotional bundles that include main courses and desserts.
f_and_b.product_role: F&B products serve as the centerpiece of family gatherings, offering convenience and exclusivity through special Eid promotions.
timing_peaks: Lunch and dinner times during Eid days

moments: Women prepare for Eid morning by applying special occasion makeup and fragrances that are marketed as part of Eid beauty collections.
Children and adults alike use new personal care products as part of their Eid preparations, ensuring they look and feel fresh for family visits.
Gift sets of perfumes and skincare products are exchanged among family members, often purchased during pre-Eid sales.
beauty_personal_care.product_role: Beauty and personal care products enhance the celebratory mood by offering special Eid-themed collections and gift sets.

moments: Families visit malls and retail outlets in the days before Eid to purchase gifts and home decor items that are part of Eid promotions.
Children eagerly anticipate receiving Eidiya, which they often spend on toys and games available in special Eid retail bundles.
Households purchase decorative items to refresh their homes for hosting Eid gatherings.
retail_lifestyle.product_role: Retail products provide the means for gift-giving and home enhancement, with special promotions driving pre-Eid shopping.

moments: Families dress in new traditional outfits for Eid prayers, often purchased from Eid-specific fashion collections.
Young adults participate in social media challenges showcasing their Eid outfits, leveraging interactive storytelling from fashion brands.
Parents shop for children's Eid clothes, ensuring they have new outfits for family gatherings and visits.
fashion.product_role: Fashion products define the visual celebration of Eid, with new collections offering style and cultural relevance.

moments: Families stock up on wellness products to ensure they stay healthy during the festive period, especially after the fasting month.
Parents purchase vitamins and supplements for children to boost their energy for the busy Eid days.
Individuals visit wellness centers for pre-Eid relaxation treatments, such as massages or spa days.
healthcare_wellness.product_role: Healthcare and wellness products support the physical well-being of individuals, ensuring they are energized and healthy for Eid festivities.

moments: Families consider moving to new homes or renovating existing ones in preparation for hosting large Eid gatherings.
Real estate agencies promote properties with spacious living areas ideal for hosting family events during Eid.
Homeowners look for short-term rental properties to accommodate visiting family members during the Eid holiday.

themes: live esports tournament coverage, team rivalries, and match highlights
Saudi Arabia's gaming-nation identity under Vision 2030 and the Saudi Esports Federation
gaming setup, gear, and lifestyle content
youth culture and the intersection of gaming with fashion and F&B
Saudi and Arab team representation and national pride
fan experiences, viewing parties, and crowd energy
dos: use high-energy, fast-paced visual editing — EWC audiences expect content that matches the pace of competitive gaming
reference specific games and tournaments (League of Legends, Valorant, FC25, PUBG, etc.) by name for credibility
show Saudi fan pride and Arab team support prominently — national identity drives the deepest engagement in this window
leverage influencer partnerships with Saudi gaming content creators who have established EWC audiences
create watch-party and group experience content — EWC is a social experience, not just an online event
use gaming-native visual language: scoreboard aesthetics, reaction clips, bracket graphics, player POV
donts: do NOT post generic gaming content without referencing EWC specifically if you want to ride the occasion
do NOT use an older demographic tone or visuals — the core EWC audience is 16-28; corporate-stiff content is actively mocked
do NOT ignore the competitive dimension — inspirational-lifestyle gaming content misses the tournament-energy of EWC; this is competition, not casual play
do NOT pretend to be present at EWC if your brand has no activation — the gaming community is highly attuned to inauthentic association
do NOT use food or lifestyle content with no gaming tie-in during EWC peak weeks — even F&B brands need the gaming contextual hook
do NOT post during match finals without tying content directly to the match moment — any off-topic post during a grand final gets buried and looks tone-deaf
esports_world_cup.commercial_note: high — peak activation for brands targeting Saudi male youth 16-28; gaming peripherals, F&B, energy drinks, fashion

Collecting exclusive memorabilia from favorite teams or players.

themes: Saudi Arabia's identity as Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques and global Islamic host
hospitality for 2M+ pilgrims from around the world — service excellence and national pride
Eid Al-Adha preparation and sacrifice rituals (Udhiya)
prayer, spiritual reflection, and du'a content
family gatherings around Eid Al-Adha that coincide with Hajj conclusion
charity and meat distribution (sadaqah) connected to Udhiya
dos: adopt a deeply reverent and humble tone — Hajj is the most spiritually weighty occasion in the Saudi calendar
if your brand has hospitality, transport, or service activations for pilgrims, document them with genuine warmth and zero promotional language
honor the Eid Al-Adha dimension — families gathering, Udhiya ritual, generosity with meat distribution
use imagery of Makkah and Al-Masjid Al-Haram only with the highest dignity and care; never crop or stylize sacred landmarks for commercial effect
share content that emphasizes national service pride — Saudi Arabia serving the Ummah is a deeply felt national identity
produce Arabic-primary copy; this occasion demands classical Arabic gravity, not colloquial lightness
donts: do NOT use Hajj imagery for promotional purposes — no discount banners, no "Hajj Sale" framing, no commercial overlays on sacred imagery
do NOT post light-hearted, humorous, or entertainment-oriented content during the core Hajj days (8-13 Dhul Hijjah)
do NOT trivialize or over-simplify the religious significance of the pilgrimage rituals (Tawaf, Sa'i, Wuquf at Arafah, etc.)
do NOT show images of pilgrims in unflattering, vulnerable, or chaotic situations — the dignity of pilgrims must be preserved
do NOT conflate Hajj Season with a generic "Muslim holiday" — the specific rituals and the Saudi hosting role are distinct and must be honored
do NOT post commercial content during the peak spiritual days without a genuine service or charity framing
hajj_season.commercial_note: medium — commercial activity is secondary; brands active in Makkah/Madinah hospitality, pilgrim services, F&B, and transportation sectors see the primary activation window

moments: Local restaurants preparing special menus for pilgrims, offering traditional Saudi dishes with a focus on easy digestion and nutrition — WHO: restaurant chefs and staff, WHEN: throughout the Hajj season
Food trucks stationed near pilgrimage sites providing quick, halal snacks and beverages — WHO: food truck operators, WHEN: midday during the Tawaf and Sa'i rituals
Families gathering for Eid Al-Adha feasts, sharing Udhiya meat with neighbors and the needy — WHO: Saudi families, WHEN: Eid Al-Adha day
f_and_b.product_role: F&B provides nourishment and comfort to pilgrims and locals, enhancing the spiritual journey.

moments: Pilgrims purchasing travel-sized hygiene kits at airports and hotels — WHO: international pilgrims, WHEN: upon arrival in Saudi Arabia
Local women preparing henna designs for Eid Al-Adha celebrations — WHO: Saudi women, WHEN: the night before Eid
Pharmacies offering skincare products to protect against the harsh sun during outdoor rituals — WHO: pharmacists and pilgrims, WHEN: midday during Hajj rituals
beauty_personal_care.product_role: Beauty and personal care ensure pilgrims maintain hygiene and comfort amidst the physical demands of Hajj.

moments: Souvenir shops in Makkah and Madinah bustling with pilgrims buying prayer beads and Islamic books — WHO: pilgrims, WHEN: post-Hajj rituals
Shopping malls offering discounts on traditional attire and gifts for Eid Al-Adha — WHO: local and visiting shoppers, WHEN: days leading up to Eid
Mobile vendors selling prayer mats and Ihram garments near pilgrimage sites — WHO: local vendors, WHEN: throughout the Hajj season
retail_lifestyle.product_role: Retail provides meaningful mementos and essentials for pilgrims, enhancing their spiritual experience.

moments: Tailors busy with last-minute alterations of Ihram garments for pilgrims — WHO: local tailors, WHEN: just before Hajj starts
Families shopping for new clothes for Eid Al-Adha celebrations — WHO: Saudi families, WHEN: week leading up to Eid
Designers showcasing modest fashion collections inspired by Hajj themes — WHO: fashion designers, WHEN: throughout the Hajj season
fashion.product_role: Fashion reflects cultural and religious identity, offering both practicality and celebration.

moments: Medical staff on high alert at clinics near pilgrimage sites, ready to assist with heat exhaustion and other ailments — WHO: healthcare workers, WHEN: peak pilgrimage days
Pilgrims receiving vaccinations and health checks before embarking on Hajj — WHO: healthcare providers, WHEN: weeks before Hajj
Wellness centers offering relaxation and recovery services post-Hajj — WHO: wellness practitioners, WHEN: after the completion of Hajj
healthcare_wellness.product_role: Healthcare ensures the safety and well-being of pilgrims, addressing physical and mental health needs.

moments: Hotels in Makkah and Madinah operating at full capacity, offering special Hajj packages — WHO: hotel managers, WHEN: entire Hajj season
Property owners renting out apartments to accommodate the influx of pilgrims — WHO: local landlords, WHEN: weeks leading up to Hajj
Real estate agents facilitating short-term leases for international visitors — WHO: real estate agents, WHEN: pre-Hajj season
real_estate.product_role: Real estate provides essential accommodations, ensuring comfort and accessibility for pilgrims.

themes: Red Sea coastal experiences, waterfront dining, and beach culture
summer entertainment programming and live events at King Abdulaziz park and Jeddah waterfront
youth and young family summer experiences
Jeddah's cosmopolitan identity — historic Balad district meets modern entertainment
outdoor food festivals and pop-up dining
fashion and beauty brand activations tied to summer lifestyle
dos: use coastal visual language — sea views, golden-hour on the Red Sea, breezy outdoor settings
emphasize Jeddah's unique cultural blend: Hejazi heritage motifs alongside modern entertainment
show large family and friend group outings — Jeddah Season draws multi-family group experiences
feature summer-specific products (light fabrics, cool beverages, outdoor dining) contextually within Season activations
reference the Jeddah waterfront (corniche) and known landmarks to establish authenticity and local pride
use vibrant, high-saturation summer color palettes that reflect the coastal energy
donts: do NOT confuse Jeddah Season aesthetics with Riyadh Season — Jeddah is coastal and more cosmopolitan, not desert-entertainment
do NOT ignore the heat factor — outdoor daytime content in June-August should acknowledge the summer heat context, not pretend it is spring
do NOT use beach imagery that conflicts with Saudi public decency standards (swimwear outside designated resort areas)
do NOT use generic Middle East summer stock imagery — Jeddah audiences recognize their city's distinctive visual identity
do NOT over-commercialize spiritual or heritage sites (Al-Balad / Historic Jeddah) with promotional overlays
do NOT post content misaligned with the summer entertainment spirit by leading with heavy discounts in the opening weeks
jeddah_season.commercial_note: high — Jeddah's peak tourism and entertainment window; summer staycation economy

Health-conscious individuals enjoying morning jogs along the Jeddah Corniche
Groups attending nutritional seminars and fitness classes to maintain energy during the busy season

themes: Vision 2030 technology narrative and Saudi Arabia's innovation ambitions
AI, deep tech, and startup ecosystem showcasing
thought leadership from Saudi and international speakers
brand partnership announcements and product launches timed to LEAP
behind-the-scenes booth and activation coverage
live session clips and keynote highlights
dos: produce live-event content from the floor — booth walk-throughs, product demos, real-time speaker quotes perform well
anchor copy to Vision 2030 language and Saudi National Strategy for Data and AI (NSDAI) themes
show Saudi faces and Saudi leaders prominently; LEAP is a pride-of-nation event
feature any partnership MOU announcements, deal signings, or product launches at LEAP with specific details
use professional event photography standards — LEAP is a premium B2B environment; content quality signals brand seriousness
post session recap content within 2 hours of the keynote ending for maximum relevance
donts: do NOT post generic "innovation" content without a specific technology or product tie-in — it reads as filler for a highly sophisticated audience
do NOT ignore the Saudi-specific framing — international tech brands that post LEAP content without referencing KSA ambition miss the emotional driver
do NOT use consumer-facing promotional language (discounts, deals) — LEAP audience is B2B decision-makers and investors
do NOT post low-quality photos from crowded hallways as your primary LEAP content — quality over volume at this event
do NOT miss the window: LEAP content has a 48-hour relevance peak; day-after recaps still work but week-after content is dead
do NOT use the event for shallow association — audiences can distinguish a brand that is genuinely participating from one that bought a badge for the photo

themes: family iftar gatherings (multi-generational)
charity and giving (zakat al-fitr)
post-iftar community moments
Suhoor and pre-dawn preparation
spiritual reflection and reading
preparation rituals (table-setting, hospitality)
dos: show iftar gatherings with families of multiple generations together
feature genuine traditional dishes (dates, qatayef, sambousa, harees) with Arabic naming
depict charity (zakat, gifting, food distribution) without exploiting the recipient
use warm golden-hour lighting and lantern-warm color palette in weeks 1-2
shift to celebratory-anticipatory tone in last 10 nights
respect prayer times: no posting during Maghreb prayer window (auto-quiet 18:00-18:30 AST during Ramadan)
donts: do NOT show food or drink consumption during fasting hours (daylight)
do NOT use aggressive promotional discount language in weeks 1-2 (commercial weight builds in last 10 days)
do NOT feature inappropriate clothing or mixed-gender intimate proximity
do NOT trivialize religious practices (no jokes about fasting, prayer, Quran recitation)
do NOT use 'Happy Ramadan' as the standalone caption — Ramadan is contemplative-first, celebratory in phase 3 only
do NOT depict charity recipients in identifiable or pitying framing
do NOT post timed promotions during iftar window without explicit family-friendly framing
ramadan.commercial_note: peak — F&B and retail spike 2-3x baseline

moments: Families gathering at restaurants for iftar buffets, with grandparents sharing stories from past Ramadans.
Friends meeting at cafes for post-taraweeh coffee, discussing their fasting experiences and plans for Eid.
Young adults participating in late-night sahur outings, exploring new eateries open for pre-dawn meals.

moments: Women indulging in skincare routines after breaking their fast, using hydrating masks to rejuvenate their skin.

moments: Families shopping for Ramadan decorations to create a festive home atmosphere, often involving children in the process.

moments: Women choosing modest yet stylish outfits for iftar gatherings, balancing tradition with modern fashion trends.

themes: live entertainment events, concerts, and sporting spectacles
family night-out experiences and themed zone visits
limited-edition product drops tied to Riyadh Season branding
pop-up activations and brand-sponsored event immersion
Vision 2030 narrative — Saudi Arabia as global entertainment destination
youth culture, gaming, and music festival energy
dos: lean into high-energy nighttime visuals — fireworks, neon, crowd energy, spotlit stages
use Riyadh Season official branding if your brand is a partner; align color palette with seasonal identity
create content around moments that audiences are already attending (concerts, sports, gaming events)
show families and groups of friends experiencing events together — multi-generational and cross-gender groups are appropriate here
leverage UGC-style authenticity — audiences trust experience-first content over polished brand ads in this context
post in real-time during major events; this is one of the few occasions where live/Story content outperforms feed posts
donts: do NOT post generic "Riyadh Season" graphics if your brand has no activation — audiences recognize hollow bandwagoning
do NOT use aggressive religious framing — this is an entertainment season, not a spiritual occasion
do NOT show content that conflicts with public decency standards at large mixed-gender events (physical proximity limits still apply)
do NOT ignore the 5-month arc — the season has sub-moments (opening week, Joy Awards, sporting events, closing ceremony); treat them individually
do NOT run heavy discount promotions in early weeks; the season opens with excitement, not bargain-hunting energy
do NOT use stock footage of generic concerts — Saudi audiences know Riyadh venues and will call out inauthenticity
riyadh_season.commercial_note: peak — brands see 2-4x baseline activation budgets; largest non-Ramadan ad spend season

moments: Families grabbing quick bites at food trucks before a concert starts

themes: the First Saudi State (1727 / 1139H) — three centuries of foundation
Najdi heritage (Dir'iyah, mud-brick architecture, palm groves)
traditional crafts (sadu weaving, calligraphy, palm-leaf work)
founding fathers and the Al-Saud lineage
historic dress (mishlah, agal, traditional women's dress)
dos: lean heavily on Najdi heritage — Dir'iyah palette (terracotta, palm green, sand)
feature traditional crafts authentically — sadu, calligraphy, palm-leaf weaving with real practitioners or artisan partners
use historic-aesthetic imagery (mud-brick architecture, palm tree, falcon) with respect
Arabic-primary, classical literary register acceptable here
respect the founding date — 1727 (1139H) — and the three centuries narrative
donts: do NOT confuse Founding Day with National Day — they have distinct meanings (Founding = three centuries; National = 1932 unification)
do NOT use stock 'desert + camel' clichés — Founding Day is specifically Najdi-historical
do NOT misuse the flag (same rules as National Day)
do NOT make Founding Day about modern Vision 2030 — it's about the historical foundation
do NOT use foreign historical-imagery aesthetics (e.g., American old-west, European-medieval)
saudi_founding_day.commercial_note: moderate — heritage fashion, traditional food, art and craft

moments: Families gather at local cafes in Dir'iyah for traditional Saudi breakfast after the morning parade.
Friends meet up at a popular Riyadh restaurant for a special Founding Day themed dinner menu.
Street vendors in historic districts sell traditional Saudi sweets and coffee to festival-goers.
Families enjoy a picnic with traditional Najdi dishes in the palm groves of Dir'iyah.
f_and_b.product_role: F&B offerings enhance the celebration with themed menus and traditional dishes.

moments: Women visit salons for special Founding Day henna designs featuring historical motifs.
Beauty influencers share tutorials on traditional Saudi makeup looks inspired by the era.
Families gift each other perfumes with scents reminiscent of palm and oud.
Beauty brands launch limited-edition packaging featuring Saudi heritage designs.
beauty_personal_care.product_role: Beauty products and services connect personal care to cultural heritage.

moments: Shoppers explore pop-up markets in heritage sites selling crafts like sadu and calligraphy.
Families visit malls hosting Founding Day exhibitions with interactive historical displays.
Retailers offer exclusive discounts on traditional Saudi attire and accessories.
Children participate in workshops on traditional crafts at community centers.
retail_lifestyle.product_role: Retail experiences immerse consumers in historical and cultural narratives.

moments: Fashion shows in Riyadh showcase modern interpretations of traditional Najdi clothing.
Influencers post styled looks featuring abayas and accessories inspired by 18th-century Saudi fashion.
Boutiques display collections with designs that pay homage to Saudi heritage.
Families dress in traditional attire for commemorative family portraits.
fashion.product_role: Fashion bridges past and present through design and cultural expression.

moments: Wellness centers offer special relaxation sessions using traditional Saudi herbal remedies.
Fitness classes incorporate historical Saudi music and dance for a cultural workout experience.

healthcare_wellness.product_role: Healthcare and wellness services integrate cultural elements for holistic experiences.

moments: Real estate tours highlight properties in Dir'iyah showcasing mud-brick architecture.
Developers host open houses with themes celebrating Saudi architectural heritage.
Families explore new residential projects designed with traditional Saudi elements.
Virtual reality experiences offer historical tours of iconic Saudi landmarks.
real_estate.product_role: Real estate connects modern living with historical roots through design and storytelling.

themes: national pride and Vision 2030 (without becoming politically loaded)
Saudi green palette + falcon + historic motifs
modernity meets heritage — Riyadh skyline alongside heritage sites
youth and ambition
achievement narratives (sport, science, culture, business)
dos: use the official Saudi green (#006C35) where palette permits; never tint or pastel-soften it
feature both heritage and modernity in the same piece — neither alone is the moment
highlight authentic Saudi cultural markers (thobe, ghutra, sadu pattern, falcon, Najdi or Hijazi architecture)
lead with pride and aspiration, not nostalgia
use Arabic-primary copy; English present but secondary
official slogan of the year if released by GACA / KSA gov — verify before use
donts: do NOT use stock 'desert + camel + sunset' clichés — Saudi National Day deserves specificity
do NOT politicize beyond Vision 2030 framing — National Day is celebratory, not policy debate
do NOT use the Saudi flag in casual/commercial decoration (e.g., on disposable items, party hats) — flag respect is law-level
do NOT use foreign cultural imagery in the lead frame
do NOT minimize the date with generic 'Happy Holidays' — it's a specific moment
saudi_national_day.commercial_note: high — National Day collections, hospitality bookings, fashion drops

moments: Families gathering for a festive breakfast at 9 AM, sharing traditional Saudi dishes like Jareesh and Mandi.

moments: Families visiting malls in the afternoon, exploring special National Day collections that blend modernity with heritage.

moments: Designers showcasing Saudi-inspired collections at pop-up fashion shows in the early evening.

moments: Morning yoga sessions in public parks, themed around national unity and well-being.

themes: online-first deals and app-based shopping events
self-gifting and personal treats for younger audiences
beauty and skincare haul content
fashion deal spotlights and seasonal wardrobe refreshes
countdown and flash sale mechanics
e-commerce platform-native content (swipe-to-shop, Stories links)
dos: lead with specific deal percentages and product names — 11.11 audiences are in active deal-comparison mode
use playful, self-indulgent framing — "treat yourself" content resonates strongly with the 18-30 female Saudi audience that drives 11.11 in beauty and fashion
produce app-first content — direct links to product pages, Stories with swipe-up to cart, Instagram Shopping integration
create urgency with countdown Stories in the 24 hours before November 11
partner with nano and micro Saudi beauty and fashion influencers who have high trust in the shopping recommendation space
use Arabic copy with relatable colloquial phrasing; this occasion skews younger and more casual than White Friday
donts: do NOT position 11.11 as equivalent to White Friday for a Saudi audience — it is a smaller, more niche event; overclaiming its scale looks out of touch
do NOT run a heavy 11.11 campaign if your brand's audience skews above 35 or into B2B — the occasion has low resonance outside younger consumer demographics

do NOT ignore that White Friday is only 10-14 days away — consumers are already researching for the bigger event; 11.11 messaging should acknowledge the value without undercutting your White Friday campaign
do NOT use corporate or formal brand voice — 11.11 content in KSA skews playful and casual; stiff copy kills engagement
do NOT post without a direct path to purchase — 11.11 is an action-first occasion; brand awareness content without a conversion route is wasted spend
do NOT duplicate your White Friday creative with just the date changed — audiences notice, and it signals a lack of genuine campaign planning
11_11_shopping.commercial_note: medium — growing KSA e-commerce event, particularly online-first beauty, fashion, and electronics; still secondary to White Friday (3 weeks later)

moments: Office workers placing group lunch orders at 11:30AM to take advantage of 11/11 discounts
Friends meeting at cafes around noon to discuss their morning shopping hauls and share app-based deals
Families planning a special lunch at home with delivery from favorite restaurants using exclusive 11/11 offers
f_and_b.product_role: F&B provides the fuel and social setting for discussing and enjoying shopping experiences.

moments: Young women unboxing skincare packages received in the morning, sharing first impressions on social media

beauty_personal_care.product_role: Beauty and personal care products are both the subject of self-gifting and content creation.

moments: Young adults browsing home decor apps during their morning commute, adding items to cart

Late-night impulse buys of gadgets and lifestyle products, driven by flash sales
retail_lifestyle.product_role: Retail lifestyle products are key for personal upgrades and home enhancements.

moments: Fitness enthusiasts purchasing new workout gear and supplements during morning sales

Evening relaxation with newly acquired aromatherapy products, shared on wellness blogs

Evening discussions about potential investments or relocations, sparked by special offers
real_estate.product_role: Real estate promotions entice potential buyers with exclusive 11/11 deals.

themes: countdown urgency and limited-time deal mechanics
product-reveal content with prominent discount callouts
category-specific deal spotlights (electronics, fashion, beauty, home)
sold-out and scarcity signaling
flash sale windows and time-limited flash drops
gift-for-self and gift-for-others framing
dos: lead with the discount percentage prominently — Saudi White Friday shoppers are comparison-driven; hide the deal and you lose the click
use countdown timers in Stories and Reels to create genuine urgency in the 72 hours before main sale day
release deal preview content 5-7 days before the main event to build wishlist behavior
use bright, high-contrast visuals — White Friday content should feel like an energy spike, not a brand editorial
show specific products with before/after price callouts, not vague "up to X% off" claims
leverage Instagram Shopping tags and direct-to-cart CTAs; this is the highest-converting swipe-up window of the year
donts: do NOT use vague "amazing deals" language without a specific number or product anchor — it reads as filler
do NOT post White Friday content in a tone-deaf context if a major religious or national event is happening simultaneously — check the calendar
do NOT run the same creative across the full 10-day window; rotate deals daily to maintain urgency
do NOT underdeliver on promises — if the content says "up to 70% off" and the landing page shows 10%, the brand takes lasting trust damage
do NOT use long-form storytelling or brand narrative copy during the active sale window; audiences are in purchase mode, not content-consumption mode
do NOT neglect the post-sale "thank you" content — a brief wrap-up post with sold-out signals reinforces scarcity for next year
white_friday.commercial_note: peak — Saudi Arabia's single highest retail sales event; electronics, fashion, and beauty spike 5-10x baseline

moments: Families rushing to stock up on gourmet snacks and beverages for weekend gatherings, especially on Thursday evening.
Young professionals ordering late-night takeout while they browse deals online.
Cafes offering special White Friday themed drinks to attract deal-hunters taking a break from shopping.
